evidence: Eastman has been increasingly using artificial intelligence tools and more traditional data science practices in its operations, research and development, and other areas to improve efficiency and effectiveness.

Eastman has implemented a risk-based governance approach led by a cross-functional Responsible AI Council that oversees AI use cases, sets standards, and coordinates with existing risk and compliance functions.
Eastman has been increasingly using AI tools and more traditional data science practices in its operations, research and development, and other areas to improve efficiency and effectiveness.
AI use cases, models, and third-party tools are subject to review, vendor due diligence, and contractual safeguards.

The use of AI by Eastman, its employees, or any of its third-party partners may result in unauthorized disclosure of data, which can result in reputational harm, loss of confidence, penalties, litigation costs, or legal liability.

Analyses, results, or business processes relying on AI may also be deficient, inaccurate, or biased, and the Company may fail to identify in a timely fashion, or at all, if or to the extent that is the case.
The Company maintains AI-specific policies, procedures, and general IT controls that define permitted uses, data handling requirements, human oversight, and documentation expectations.

limitations: No quantified AI investment amounts, headcount, or budget figures appear in the excerpts; investment intensity scored at 3 (named programs, no quantified scale).
AI use cases are named at a domain level (operations, R&D) but no specific deployed applications or quantified outcomes are described; concreteness capped at 3.

Risk disclosure describes specific risk mechanisms (data disclosure, bias, third-party exposure) and references a named governance body (Responsible AI Council) and AI-specific policies, supporting a score of 4, but no quantified risk metrics or formal oversight reporting structure details are provided to justify a 5.
